page.title: The Navigator
page.description: Career timeline and scope expansion: how my work moved from visual interfaces to software delivery, frontend architecture, product architecture, and a current Staff Software Engineer scope.

hero.title: The Navigator
hero.subtitle: A career timeline across different seas of engineering scope.
hero.lead: The Navigator is the evolution log: how my work moved from visual interfaces to software delivery, frontend architecture, product architecture, and Staff Software Engineer scope.
hero.intro: This page is built around one idea: each period expanded the waters I could read, the systems I could navigate, and the decisions I could help guide.

metadata.impact: Better judgment across time, systems, teams, and product constraints

labels.core: The route: the summary view, the timeline across design, engineering, frontend architecture, product architecture, and the Staff role, the shift in scope, and the current position
labels.impact: The impact of a career that progressed from interfaces to product systems

items: I started in design, where I learned to read interfaces as systems of hierarchy, structure, and intent.
I moved into engineering to understand how interfaces behave under real technical constraints.
I expanded into frontend architecture as reuse, consistency, accessibility, and testing became the main problems.
I moved toward product architecture when frontend decisions became connected to workflows, APIs, data, and teams.
My current Staff scope is the result of those stages compounding, not a separate reinvention.

items.title: 01 — Design
items.description: 2006–2012 · Interface clarity, visual hierarchy, interaction structure.

items.title: 02 — Engineering
items.description: 2012–2018 · Implementation quality, production behavior, maintainable delivery.

items.title: 03 — Frontend Architecture
items.description: 2018–2022 · Reusable systems, component patterns, accessibility, testing, standards.

items.title: 04 — Product Architecture
items.description: 2022–2024 · Product flows, data movement, API collaboration, validation, boundaries.

items.title: 05 — Staff Software Engineer
items.description: 2025–Present · Product systems, technical direction, design systems, component architecture, AI-enabled capabilities.

diagram.title: Career Navigation Timeline
diagram.description: Shows the page’s central model: each phase increases technical scope and decision consequence.
diagram.caption: The route matters because each stage expanded the type of systems I could understand and influence.
diagram.summary: A left-to-right progression: Design, then Engineering, then Frontend Architecture, then Product Architecture, then Staff Software Engineer.

primaryContent.title: Scope Shift
paragraphs: The important signal is not the number of years. It is how the work changed.

Design taught me to understand structure. Engineering taught me to make structure executable. Frontend architecture taught me to scale structure across teams. Product architecture taught me to connect structure to behavior. Staff work requires turning those lessons into direction, alignment, and durable systems.

diagram.title: Scope Expansion Model
diagram.description: Shows how responsibility moved from visible output to product-system consequence.
diagram.caption: The work expanded from local execution to decisions that affect teams, systems, and product evolution.
diagram.summary: Responsibility widened in layers: from Interface, to Feature, to Component System, to Product Flow, to Technical Direction.

primaryContent.title: Current Position
paragraphs: Today, I operate as a Staff Software Engineer with a foundation in design systems, frontend architecture, component architecture, product architecture, and engineering leadership.
AI belongs in this timeline as a current product capability, not as a separate identity. I treat it as another layer to integrate responsibly into workflows, interfaces, and product systems.

items.title: Clearer Technical Judgment
items.description: The progression gives me range: I can evaluate decisions from the interface layer down to architecture, workflow, and maintainability.

items.title: Stronger Cross-Functional Translation
items.description: I can connect design intent, engineering constraints, product behavior, and implementation trade-offs without losing context.

items.title: Safer Product Evolution
items.description: Architecture decisions become stronger when they account for how products change over time.

items.title: Credible Staff-Level Scope
items.description: The current role is supported by accumulated experience across design, delivery, architecture, systems, and team alignment.

items.title: The Server
items.description: Explains the engineering identity behind this timeline: how I think, work, and collaborate today.

items.title: The Fixer
items.description: Shows how this evolution translates into diagnosis, trade-offs, constraints, and quality.

items.title: The Integrator
items.description: Connects the current Staff scope across product needs, architecture, design systems, APIs, teams, and AI-enabled direction.

items.title: Nobody knows he worked on…
items.description: Provides the project evidence behind the timeline.

closingThought.statement: A navigator is useful because he understands the route, the constraints, and the conditions before deciding where to steer.

coverline: I'm an old sea wolf who has sailed the seven seas...
